title: Raj Rajaratnam, Galleon Group Founder, Convicted On All Counts In Insider Trading Trial (DOJ, SDNY)
bias_or_interest: Government announcement of the jury verdict. Authoritative for the conviction, the date, and the 14 counts (9 securities fraud, 5 conspiracy).
relevant_locations: Jury verdict May 11, 2011 — guilty on all 14 counts
Founder/manager of the Galleon Group hedge funds
Wiretap evidence central to the case
quotation_note: Conviction is a finding of fact (jury trial). justice.gov can block automated fetch (403); confirm verbatim quotes in a browser before locking narration.
notes: Spine of the reckoning act. Cross-check with SRC-0009 (Reuters verdict coverage).

title: Raj Rajaratnam Sentenced In Manhattan Federal Court To 11 Years In Prison (DOJ, SDNY)
bias_or_interest: Government release on the sentence. Firmest basis for 11-year term, the $53,816,434 forfeiture, and the $10 million fine.
relevant_locations: Sentenced October 13, 2011 to 132 months (11 years)
Criminal forfeiture of $53,816,434
$10 million fine
Prosecutors characterized it as the longest insider-trading sentence imposed
quotation_note: Attribute the 'longest insider-trading sentence' superlative to prosecutors/coverage at the time. Verify exact forfeiture figure verbatim.
notes: Load-bearing for penalties. Keep the superlative attributed.

title: SEC Obtains Record $92.8 Million Penalty Against Raj Rajaratnam (SEC Litigation Release / Press Release)
bias_or_interest: Government release on the civil penalty. Authoritative for the $92,805,705 figure and the SEC's 'record' characterization for an insider-trading case.
relevant_locations: Civil penalty of $92,805,705 — described by the SEC as the largest ever against an individual in an SEC insider-trading case
Entered by the U.S. District Court (SDNY) Nov 8, 2011
quotation_note: State $92.8M as the SEC civil penalty; keep it separate from the criminal forfeiture/fine. Verify the exact dollar figure.
notes: Combine with SRC-0002 for the '>$156.6M combined sanctions' figure (53.8M + 10M + 92.8M).

title: SEC Charges Billionaire Hedge Fund Manager Raj Rajaratnam With Insider Trading (SEC Press Release 2009-211)
bias_or_interest: Initial civil charges (allegations as of 2009). Authoritative for the date of the takedown and the network framing; the named tippers; AUM context.
relevant_locations: Charges announced October 16, 2009
Galleon a hedge-fund family managing roughly $7 billion
Named tippers including Rajiv Goel (Intel), Anil Kumar (McKinsey), Robert Moffat (IBM), Danielle Chiesi (New Castle)
quotation_note: 2009 charges = allegations; the 2011 jury verdict is what converts the core to fact. Use 2009 release for arrest date and AUM.
notes: Use for the Oct 16, 2009 arrest morning and the $7B AUM.

title: FBI — The Insider Trading Case Against Raj Rajaratnam / Operation Perfect Hedge (FBI feature)
bias_or_interest: Investigating agency's own account. Authoritative for the investigative method — court-authorized wiretaps applied to securities fraud, previously associated with organized crime and narcotics.
relevant_locations: First use of court-authorized wiretaps as the centerpiece of a major insider-trading case
Wiretaps a tool historically used against organized crime and drug trafficking
Agents recorded the defendants' own conversations
quotation_note: The wiretap-as-method framing is well supported; the 'first major insider case built on wiretaps' should still be phrased as widely reported / according to the FBI and prosecutors.
notes: Cinematic core: the voice on the wire. Cross-check SRC-0010 (NYT).

title: United States v. Rajaratnam, 719 F.3d 139 (2d Cir. 2013) — appeals court upholds the wiretaps
organization: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit
bias_or_interest: Primary court opinion. Authoritative that the conviction was affirmed and the wiretap evidence held lawfully obtained and admissible.
relevant_locations: Second Circuit affirms the conviction (2013)
Wiretap authorization upheld as lawful; evidence admissible
Establishes wiretaps as a usable tool in insider-trading prosecutions
quotation_note: Primary legal authority — safe to state the appeals court upheld the wiretaps as lawful in 2013.
notes: Anchors the legal-legitimacy beat in the reckoning.

title: Rajat Gupta Sentenced In Manhattan Federal Court To Two Years In Prison (DOJ, SDNY)
bias_or_interest: Government release on a SEPARATE defendant. Authoritative for Gupta's June 2012 conviction (one conspiracy + three securities-fraud counts), 2-year sentence, and $5M fine.
relevant_locations: Rajat Gupta — former Goldman Sachs director and ex-McKinsey global head
Convicted June 2012 of one conspiracy count and three securities-fraud counts for tipping Rajaratnam
Sentenced to 2 years in prison and a $5 million fine
quotation_note: STRICT: keep Gupta's conduct and penalties separate from Rajaratnam's. Gupta is a distinct case.
notes: Used only to show the network reached the Goldman boardroom — never merged with Raj's numbers.

title: SEC Charges in the Galleon Insider Trading Network — overview of defendants charged (SEC)
bias_or_interest: SEC overview of the broader sweep. Supports the scope: the wider Galleon probe led to civil charges against roughly two dozen-plus individuals and entities.
relevant_locations: Galleon-related SEC charges against 29 individuals and entities
Parallel criminal cases producing dozens of convictions/guilty pleas across the probe
quotation_note: State '29 individuals and entities (SEC)' and 'dozens of convictions across the wider probe' as attributed to the SEC/press tallies.
notes: Scope beat: the case was bigger than one man.

title: Rajaratnam found guilty in hedge fund insider trading case (Reuters)
author: Grant McCool; Jonathan Stempel
bias_or_interest: Major wire-service trial coverage. Independent confirmation of the verdict, the courtroom scene, and the wiretap's role.
relevant_locations: Guilty on all 14 counts, May 11, 2011
Largest hedge-fund insider-trading case of its kind at the time
Wiretapped phone calls played for the jury
quotation_note: Independent corroboration of the DOJ release; good for human/courtroom detail.
rights_note: News report — paraphrase; do not reproduce long verbatim passages on screen.

title: In Galleon Case, a Verdict Won on Wiretaps (The New York Times)
author: Peter Lattman; Azam Ahmed
bias_or_interest: Major-outlet analysis emphasizing that the wiretaps — not paper trails — won the case, and that the method was borrowed from organized-crime prosecutions.
relevant_locations: Wiretaps were the decisive evidence
Method historically used against the mob and drug rings
Significance as a precedent for future insider-trading cases
quotation_note: Supports the 'heard the crime in their own words' thesis. Paraphrase.
notes: Thematic anchor for the surveillance movement.

title: SEC and DOJ actions against Galleon tippers — Rajiv Goel (Intel), Anil Kumar (McKinsey), Robert Moffat (IBM), Danielle Chiesi (New Castle)
organization: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission / DOJ — SDNY
bias_or_interest: Government actions identifying the corporate-insider tippers and their employers. Authoritative for who fed information from where.
relevant_locations: Rajiv Goel — Intel Capital
Anil Kumar — McKinsey & Company
Robert Moffat — IBM senior executive
Danielle Chiesi — New Castle Funds
quotation_note: State each tipper's source company precisely; charged separately. Goel and Kumar cooperated; describe roles carefully.
notes: Used to build the whisper-network act without conflating defendants.

title: Galleon Group profile — assets under management and Rajaratnam's standing (Bloomberg / Forbes coverage)
bias_or_interest: Business-press context for Galleon's scale (~$7B at peak) and Rajaratnam's billionaire standing.
relevant_locations: Galleon managed roughly $7 billion at its peak
Rajaratnam ranked among the wealthiest Americans before the case
Galleon wound down after the charges
quotation_note: Use for AUM and wealth context; corroborate $7B with SRC-0004 framing.
notes: Background color for the rise act.

title: Raj Rajaratnam released from prison to home confinement (Reuters / Bloomberg, First Step Act)
bias_or_interest: Coverage of his 2019 release to home confinement under the First Step Act after serving the bulk of his term.
relevant_locations: Released to home confinement in 2019
First Step Act early-release provisions
Served the majority of the 11-year term
quotation_note: State 'released to home confinement in 2019' as reported; do not overstate remaining-term specifics.

title: Raj Rajaratnam memoir 'Uneven Justice: The Plot to Sink Galleon' (his own account)
author: Raj Rajaratnam
bias_or_interest: Subject's own self-interested account; maintains he was wronged. Usable ONLY as 'in his memoir, he says...' — never as a neutral fact source.
relevant_locations: Rajaratnam published a memoir (~2021) framing his prosecution as unjust
He continued to dispute aspects of the case
quotation_note: VERIFY title, publisher, and date before citing on air. Attribute strictly to him; it is not evidence of innocence.
rights_note: Quote sparingly; attribute. Verify before use.
notes: Grade C / conditional. Lets the film acknowledge his side without endorsing it.

title: How the wiretap began: the cooperating witness Roomy Khan and the path to the Galleon wiretap (NYT / Reuters reconstructions)
bias_or_interest: Major-outlet reconstruction of how investigators built probable cause and obtained the wiretap authorization, including the role of a cooperating witness.
relevant_locations: A cooperating witness helped establish the basis for the wiretap
Investigators escalated from informants to a court-authorized phone tap
The first recorded calls turned suspicion into hard evidence
quotation_note: Use to dramatize the investigative escalation; keep procedural specifics attributed to reporting.
notes: Bridges the suspicion act into the wiretap act.
